learningOutcomes: Explain Mendel's laws of inheritance
Distinguish between dominant and recessive traits
Construct and interpret simple Punnett squares
realLifeContexts: Why children don't always look like their parents
Predicting blood type inheritance
Why some traits skip generations
commonMisconceptions: Dominant traits are always more common in a population
Offspring are always a blend of parent traits
One gene always controls one trait

learningOutcomes: Apply dihybrid cross and the law of independent assortment
Explain codominance, incomplete dominance, and multiple alleles
Understand sex-linked inheritance and X-linked traits
Interpret pedigree charts
realLifeContexts: ABO blood group system as multiple alleles and codominance
Why colorblindness is more common in males (X-linked)
Genetic counseling using pedigree analysis
commonMisconceptions: Sex-linked traits are only passed from father to son
Codominance and incomplete dominance are the same
A carrier always shows some symptoms of a recessive condition

learningOutcomes: Explain chromosomal theory of inheritance and linkage
Analyze mutation types and their effects
Understand chromosomal abnormalities (Down syndrome, Turner syndrome)
Apply Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium to population genetics
realLifeContexts: Genetic testing for chromosomal abnormalities in prenatal diagnosis
Population genetics in conservation biology
How gene mapping led to discovery of disease genes
commonMisconceptions: Linked genes never separate
All mutations are harmful
Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium describes real evolving populations

learningOutcomes: Describe the structure and function of major organ systems
Explain digestion, circulation, and respiration at a basic level
Identify major organs and their roles
realLifeContexts: Why we breathe faster during exercise
How food is broken down and nutrients absorbed
Why kidneys are vital for survival
commonMisconceptions: Blood is blue inside the body
The heart pumps blood only to the lungs
Digestion starts in the stomach

learningOutcomes: Explain mechanisms of gas exchange, nerve impulse transmission, and hormonal regulation
Describe the cardiac cycle and blood pressure regulation
Explain kidney filtration and urine formation
Understand immune response and vaccination
realLifeContexts: How vaccines create immunity without causing disease
Why altitude affects breathing (reduced O2 partial pressure)
How diabetes results from insulin dysregulation
commonMisconceptions: Arteries always carry oxygenated blood
The brain controls all functions consciously
More urine always means healthier kidneys

learningOutcomes: Analyze feedback mechanisms in homeostasis
Explain the molecular basis of nerve conduction and muscle contraction
Evaluate the interplay between nervous and endocrine systems
Assess physiological responses to disease and stress
realLifeContexts: How anesthesia works at the molecular level
Why autoimmune diseases occur (self vs. non-self recognition failure)
Physiological basis of stress and cortisol
commonMisconceptions: Nerve impulses travel as electrical current like in wires
Hormones only affect target organs and have no side effects
Negative feedback always means decreasing a response

learningOutcomes: Explain photosynthesis and respiration in plants
Describe how water and minerals are absorbed and transported
Identify the role of leaves, roots, and stems
realLifeContexts: Why plants need sunlight and water to grow
Why leaves wilt when plants are dehydrated
How forests affect local rainfall (transpiration)
commonMisconceptions: Plants only perform photosynthesis, not respiration
Plants get food from the soil
All water movement in plants is upward

learningOutcomes: Explain the light and dark reactions of photosynthesis
Describe mineral nutrition and deficiency symptoms
Understand plant growth regulators and their roles
Explain mechanisms of transpiration and water potential
realLifeContexts: How ripening is controlled commercially using ethylene
Why nitrogen deficiency causes yellowing leaves
How herbicides work by blocking photosynthesis pathways
commonMisconceptions: The Calvin cycle requires light
Auxins always promote growth in all plant parts
Transpiration is wasteful and serves no function

learningOutcomes: Compare C3, C4, and CAM photosynthetic pathways
Analyze the molecular mechanisms of signal transduction in plants
Evaluate adaptations to stress (drought, salinity, cold)
Explain nitrogen fixation and its ecological significance
realLifeContexts: Why C4 crops (maize, sugarcane) are more efficient in hot climates
How drought-resistant crops are engineered using ABA signaling
Rhizobium symbiosis reducing need for synthetic fertilizers
commonMisconceptions: C4 plants don't perform the Calvin cycle
Nitrogen fixation can be done by all microorganisms
Photorespiration is only wasteful with no adaptive value

learningOutcomes: Explain Darwin's theory of natural selection
Define adaptation and give examples
Understand the concept of common ancestry
realLifeContexts: Why bacteria become resistant to antibiotics
How the peppered moth changed color during industrialization
Why humans and chimps share similar DNA
commonMisconceptions: Evolution means humans came from monkeys
Organisms evolve to survive — it is not intentional
Evolution always produces more complex organisms

learningOutcomes: Distinguish between different types of natural selection
Explain mechanisms of speciation (allopatric, sympatric)
Interpret evidence for evolution from fossils, anatomy, and molecular biology
Describe genetic drift, gene flow, and mutation as evolutionary forces
realLifeContexts: Galápagos finch beak variation as speciation evidence
How island populations diverge rapidly
Vestigial structures in humans (coccyx, wisdom teeth)
commonMisconceptions: Genetic drift is the same as natural selection
Speciation always takes millions of years
Homologous structures prove the same function in different organisms

learningOutcomes: Apply Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium to test for evolution
Analyze phylogenetic trees and molecular clocks
Evaluate competing theories of evolution (gradualism vs. punctuated equilibrium)
Discuss co-evolution, sexual selection, and kin selection
realLifeContexts: How molecular clocks date divergence of HIV strains for epidemiology
Predator-prey co-evolution arms races
Sexual selection in bird-of-paradise plumage
commonMisconceptions: Molecular clocks are perfectly accurate
Phylogenetic trees show who evolved from whom directly
Sexual selection always opposes natural selection

learningOutcomes: Define biotechnology and give everyday examples
Explain what DNA is and why it is used in biotechnology
Describe basic applications: fermentation, vaccines, and GM crops
realLifeContexts: How insulin is produced using bacteria (GM bacteria)
Why GM crops can resist pests
How fermentation makes yogurt, cheese, and bread
commonMisconceptions: GMO foods contain active foreign DNA that changes the human genome
Cloning means making an exact personality copy
All fermentation produces alcohol

learningOutcomes: Explain recombinant DNA technology step-by-step
Describe PCR and gel electrophoresis and their applications
Understand gene expression systems in biotechnology
Evaluate ethical issues around GMOs and cloning
realLifeContexts: Forensic DNA fingerprinting in crime investigation
CRISPR editing to cure sickle cell disease
Production of hepatitis B vaccine in yeast
commonMisconceptions: PCR amplifies only the gene of interest without errors
CRISPR can be used to edit any gene without off-target effects
Recombinant proteins are always identical to natural proteins

learningOutcomes: Analyze gene therapy strategies and delivery challenges
Evaluate CRISPR-Cas9 mechanism, applications, and ethical concerns
Explain stem cell technology and regenerative medicine
Assess bioinformatics tools in genomics and proteomics
realLifeContexts: CRISPR trials to cure beta-thalassemia in humans
Stem cell therapy for Parkinson's and spinal cord injury
Bioinformatics in COVID-19 vaccine development
commonMisconceptions: Gene therapy permanently cures all genetic diseases
iPSCs are ethically uncontroversial because they don't use embryos
CRISPR edits are always permanent and heritable

learningOutcomes: Define ecosystem, biotic, and abiotic components
Explain food chains and food webs
Describe the role of producers, consumers, and decomposers
realLifeContexts: Why removing wolves from Yellowstone changed the river course (trophic cascade)
How deforestation affects water cycles
Why bees are essential for food production
commonMisconceptions: Food chains are straight lines in nature
Decomposers are not important to ecosystems
Humans are at the top of all food chains

learningOutcomes: Explain energy flow through trophic levels and ecological efficiency
Describe biogeochemical cycles (carbon, nitrogen, water)
Understand population dynamics and carrying capacity
Explain ecological succession and climax community
realLifeContexts: Why eating lower on the food chain is more energy-efficient
How deforestation contributes to climate change (carbon cycle)
Rewilding projects restoring ecological succession
commonMisconceptions: The 10% rule means exactly 10% always transfers
Carrying capacity is a fixed number for any species
Climax communities are the end of all succession everywhere

learningOutcomes: Analyze ecosystem services and their economic valuation
Evaluate biodiversity indices and conservation strategies
Apply metapopulation theory to habitat fragmentation
Assess the impact of climate change on ecosystem structure
realLifeContexts: REDD+ carbon credit programs for forest conservation
How habitat corridors help tiger conservation in India
Ocean acidification effects on coral reef biodiversity
commonMisconceptions: Higher biodiversity always means more stable ecosystems
Keystone species are always predators
Island biogeography only applies to actual islands

learningOutcomes: Compare prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells
Identify cell organelles and their functions
Explain cell membrane structure and transport
realLifeContexts: Why bacteria are easier to kill with antibiotics than viruses
How cancer cells differ from normal cells
Why plant cells have cell walls and animal cells don't
commonMisconceptions: All cells have a nucleus
The cell membrane is a rigid barrier
Mitochondria only produce energy with no other functions

learningOutcomes: Explain the cell cycle, mitosis, and meiosis in detail
Describe protein synthesis (transcription and translation)
Understand active transport, endocytosis, and exocytosis
Explain cell signaling and signal transduction pathways
realLifeContexts: How cancer develops from uncontrolled mitosis
How HIV infects cells using receptor-mediated endocytosis
How nerve cells use signal transduction to respond to stimuli
commonMisconceptions: Meiosis only occurs in sex organs
mRNA is the same as DNA
Signal transduction always results in gene expression changes

learningOutcomes: Analyze mechanisms of cell cycle regulation and checkpoints
Explain apoptosis and its role in development and disease
Evaluate the role of post-translational modification
Assess stem cell differentiation and epigenetics
realLifeContexts: How p53 mutations lead to cancer development
How epigenetic changes in development are transmitted across generations
Targeted cancer therapy using CDK inhibitors
commonMisconceptions: p53 directly kills cancer cells
Apoptosis and necrosis are both forms of cell death with the same outcome
Epigenetic changes never affect the DNA sequence but always change gene expression

learningOutcomes: Distinguish between sexual and asexual reproduction
Describe the human reproductive system
Explain fertilization and early development
realLifeContexts: Why sexual reproduction produces more variation than asexual
How IVF helps couples with infertility
Why identical twins share the same DNA
commonMisconceptions: Identical twins are always the same in every way
Asexual reproduction is only found in plants
Fertilization always occurs in the uterus

learningOutcomes: Explain the hormonal control of the menstrual cycle
Describe gametogenesis (spermatogenesis and oogenesis)
Understand implantation, placental function, and parturition
Compare reproductive strategies across different organisms
realLifeContexts: How contraceptives work by disrupting hormonal cycles
How the placenta provides fetal nutrition and immunity
Differences in reproductive strategies: r vs. K selection
commonMisconceptions: Ovulation always occurs on day 14 of the cycle
The placenta is part of the fetus
Hormonal contraceptives cause infertility long-term

learningOutcomes: Analyze the molecular signals controlling implantation and pregnancy
Evaluate assisted reproductive technologies and their limitations
Explain parthenogenesis and polyploidy in plants
Assess the evolutionary advantages of sexual vs. asexual reproduction
realLifeContexts: How pregnancy tests detect HCG
Polyploidy in crop plants (wheat having 6 genome copies)
Evolutionary benefits of sex in rapidly changing environments
commonMisconceptions: HCG is produced by the embryo immediately after fertilization
Polyploidy always results in larger, healthier organisms
Parthenogenesis only occurs in insects
